excerpt: The proof-of-work involves scanning for a value that when hashed begins with a number of zero bits. The average work required is exponential in the number of zero bits required and can be verified by executing a single hash. Once the CPU effort has been expended, the block cannot be changed without redoing the work.
body:
Source excerpt:
The proof-of-work involves scanning for a value that when hashed begins with a number of zero bits. The average work required is exponential in the number of zero bits required and can be verified by executing a single hash. Once the CPU effort has been expended, the block cannot be changed without redoing the work.

---

Cited by leaf "Why proof of work":
Proof of work is the rule that says: to extend the chain, you must show evidence that a measurable amount of real-world energy was spent doing it. The work is finding a block header whose double-SHA256 hash is below the current difficulty target — there is no shortcut, you grind random nonces until one fits [1]. The output is cheap to verify (one hash) and expensive to produce (trillions of hashes per block at current difficulty), which is exactly the asymmetry Bitcoin uses to gate writes to its ledger.

The property this buys is unforgeable costliness, in Nick Szabo's phrase. An attacker who wants to rewrite the past has to redo all the work that produced it, faster than the honest network is producing new work — that's what "51% attack" means in practice [2]. The cost is external: it shows up as electricity bills and depreciating hardware, denominated in something the protocol doesn't control. Proof of stake, by contrast, secures the chain with the same asset the chain itself issues, which makes the security cost endogenous and the slashing rules a matter of social consensus rather than physics.

The other thing PoW gets you is permissionless Sybil resistance. There is no validator set, no whitelist, no minimum balance — anyone with a power outlet and an ASIC can start contributing hashrate, and their share of block production is exactly proportional to their share of the work. That property is what makes the network resistant to capture by entities whose names you'd recognize: even a state-level adversary has to outspend everyone else in the world combined, on hardware that is itself a competitive market [3].

---

Cited by leaf "Hashcash (Adam Back, 1997)":
Adam Back proposed Hashcash in 1997 as an anti-spam measure. The construction: to send an email, attach a token consisting of the recipient's address, the date, and a nonce, such that the SHA1 hash of the whole token has a configurable number of leading zero bits [1]. Verifying the token is one hash operation; producing one requires brute-forcing nonces until the hash falls below the target, which on consumer hardware took a few seconds for a reasonable difficulty. A normal sender pays a few CPU-seconds per email; a spammer trying to send a million pays a CPU-month.

The primitive Bitcoin reuses is the difficulty-adjustable hash puzzle: a specific message must hash below a threshold, the threshold parameterizes the cost, and verification is trivial. Bitcoin substitutes SHA256 for SHA1, applies it to the block header instead of an email token, and uses a difficulty that targets ten-minute production rather than seconds. The Hashcash mechanism is referenced explicitly in Section 4 of the Bitcoin whitepaper [2].

What Hashcash didn't try to do — and Bitcoin does — is convert the work into a transferable, cumulative scarcity. A Hashcash token is an admission ticket for one specific email; once redeemed it has no further value, and there's no way to compose multiple tokens into a record of who has done how much work. Bitcoin's chain-of-blocks structure turns proof-of-work from a single-use anti-spam tax into a running ledger where the cost of rewriting history compounds with every block added [3]. The cryptographic primitive is the same; the architectural use is different.

---

Cited by leaf "The 2008 whitepaper":
Satoshi's October 2008 paper, "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System," is nine pages and eight references. It opens by stating the problem it claims to solve — payments online still depend on trusted intermediaries — and proposes a system where transactions are timestamped into a chain of proof-of-work blocks that nodes accept by extending the longest chain they see [1]. The technical content is mostly description of pieces that already existed; the contribution is the assembly.

The references list is the cleanest summary of what the paper draws on: Wei Dai's b-money for the idea of a distributed monetary system gated by computation; Adam Back's Hashcash for the specific proof-of-work primitive; Haber and Stornetta's 1991 timestamping work for the chain-of-hashes structure; and Massias-Avila-Quisquater for the related linked-timestamp construction [2]. Notably absent from the citations: Nick Szabo's Bit Gold (which Szabo says he hadn't published in a citable form by 2008) and Hal Finney's RPOW (a working precursor). What Bitcoin adds on top is the use of proof-of-work for both Sybil-resistance among writers and convergence on a single history — and the issuance schedule that pays the writers in the same money the system itself secures.

The parts that are sometimes overstated: the paper does not introduce proof-of-work, distributed timestamping, public-key digital cash, peer-to-peer networking, or the idea of a fixed monetary supply. The parts that are sometimes understated: the specific combination — open issuance to anyone who does the work, longest-chain rule for convergence, no privileged identities, no trusted setup — was new, and the design has been running uninterrupted since January 2009 with no successful attack on the consensus rules. The whitepaper is an engineering document about a working prototype, not a manifesto; the political claims people now associate with Bitcoin came later, mostly from the readers [3].
